title: The connection between the gut-brain axis and anxiety
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The knot in your gut isn't just in your head

You know that feeling. The one that hits you right in the stomach before a hard conversation, a panic attack, or even just waking up in the morning with dread already sitting there like a stone. That knot. That churn. That hollow, sick sensation that makes you want to curl into a ball and disappear.

For years, I thought my gut was just reacting to my anxiety. That my stomach was this innocent bystander, getting punched by my overactive brain. But here's the thing: the research is showing it's the other way around. Or at least, it's a two-way street. And that street is called the gut-brain axis.

It's not just metaphor. Your gut and your brain are physically connected by the vagus nerve, a massive superhighway of neurons that runs from your brainstem down to your abdomen. Think of it as a direct phone line between your emotional center and your digestive system. And right now, for many of us with complex trauma, that line is ringing off the hook with distress signals.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote the book on how trauma lives in the body, has talked extensively about this. He points out that the gut has its own nervous system, the enteric nervous system, which is sometimes called the "second brain." It has about 100 million neurons. That's more than your spinal cord. Stay with me here: your gut can literally think and feel on its own, and it's constantly sending messages up to your brain about whether the world is safe or not.

When you have a history of trauma, your gut doesn't just relax and digest. It stays on high alert. It's like a security guard who's been through too many break-ins and now sees a threat in every shadow. That's not just anxiety in your head. That's your gut screaming at your brain, "Danger! Danger! Do something!"

How your microbiome talks to your amygdala

Here's where it gets wild. Your gut is home to trillions of bacteria, fungi, and other microorganisms that make up your microbiome. And these little guys are not just passive passengers. They are actively producing neurotransmitters that directly affect your mood, your fear responses, and your ability to feel calm.

Stay with me here. I know this can feel like a lot. The science, the terminology, the implications. But underneath all of it is something simple: your body has been trying to keep you alive. Every symptom you have - the anxiety, the dissociation, the hypervigilance, the numbness - those are survival strategies. They worked. They got you here.

Pat Ogden's sensorimotor psychotherapy approach is built on this understanding. The body isn't the enemy. It's not something to override or push through. It's the primary pathway back to yourself. When we listen to what the body is doing - really listen, without judgment - we start to understand the logic of our own survival.

Right now, as you read this, can you notice your feet on the ground? Can you feel the weight of your body in the chair? That's not a small thing. That's your nervous system practicing presence. Practicing being here, in this moment, instead of back there in the memory. Every time you do that - every single time - you're building new neural pathways. You're proving to your body that now is different from then.

About 90 to 95 percent of your body's serotonin, the neurotransmitter that helps you feel stable and happy, is produced in your gut. Not in your brain. In your gut. So when your microbiome is out of balance, which is incredibly common in people with chronic stress and trauma, your serotonin production can tank. And that leaves you more vulnerable to anxiety, depression, and that raw, on-edge feeling that never quite goes away.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has written about how the body's innate survival responses get stuck in trauma. And your gut is a major player in that stuckness. Think about it: when an animal in the wild escapes a predator, it shakes off the excess energy and then its digestive system goes back to normal. But for humans with complex trauma, that shaking never fully happens. The gut stays locked in a state of freeze or hypervigilance, constantly sending alarm signals up the vagus nerve to the amygdala, your brain's fear center.

I remember sitting with a client once who described her anxiety as "a swarm of bees in my stomach." She had tried every breathing exercise, every meditation app, every positive affirmation. Nothing touched it. Because the bees weren't in her head. They were in her gut. And until we started working with her digestive system, her nervous system, and her microbiome together, the anxiety kept coming back. Right?

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's an amino acid that can help calm the nervous system without sedation, and many people find it supports that gut-brain connection in a subtle but real way.

Inflammation, leaky gut, and the anxiety feedback loop

Here's something they don't tell you in most anxiety recovery spaces. Chronic stress and trauma can actually damage the lining of your gut. This condition, often called "leaky gut" or increased intestinal permeability, means that tiny particles of food, bacteria, and toxins can slip through your gut wall into your bloodstream. Your immune system sees these as invaders and launches an inflammatory response.

Inflammation is not just about joint pain or allergies. It directly affects your brain. Inflammatory cytokines can cross the blood-brain barrier and trigger what researchers call "sickness behavior." That's the exact same constellation of symptoms as anxiety: fatigue, social withdrawal, heightened startle response, difficulty concentrating, and that deep sense of unease. Your body is literally acting like it's fighting an infection, even when there's no virus or bacteria to fight.

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who coined the term "interpersonal neurobiology," talks about how the brain and body are a single integrated system. You can't treat anxiety by just working on your thoughts if your gut is inflamed and your immune system is screaming. It's like trying to fix a leaky pipe by painting the wall. You have to go to the source.

And here's the hard truth: the wellness industry loves to sell you supplements and protocols for gut health. But if you have complex trauma, your gut issues are often driven by your nervous system being stuck in survival mode. No amount of kombucha and probiotics will fix a vagus nerve that's locked in freeze. You have to address the trauma at the same time.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk lays out the science of how trauma lives in the body, including the gut, and offers a roadmap for healing that goes beyond just talk therapy.

Practical steps for calming the gut-brain connection

So what do you actually do about this? I'm not going to give you a rigid protocol or a ten-step plan. That's not how this works. But there are a few things that can help shift the system, and they're all about signaling safety to your nervous system, not just to your thoughts.

First, vagus nerve stimulation is real and it works. Simple things like humming, singing, gargling, or even laughing can activate the vagus nerve and tell your gut to calm down. I know it sounds ridiculous. But there's actual research showing that these activities increase heart rate variability, which is a marker of a flexible, resilient nervous system. Try humming a song for thirty seconds and notice what happens in your stomach. Seriously. Try it right now.

Think about it this way. Your brain developed in an environment where certain things were true. Maybe love came with conditions. Maybe safety was unpredictable. Maybe the people who were supposed to protect you were the same people your nervous system needed protection from. That creates a particular kind of wiring.

Sebern Fisher's work on neurofeedback shows how these patterns literally show up in brainwave activity. Hypervigilant brains produce excess high-beta waves. Dissociative brains show too much slow-wave activity in the wrong places. This isn't metaphor. This is measurable, observable, and - critically - changeable.

I remember the first time I saw my own brain map. Seeing the dysregulation in color-coded images on a screen. Something shifted in that moment. It wasn't shame. It was relief. Because if it's in the wiring, then it's not a character flaw. It's not weakness. It's not something I chose. And if it got wired this way through experience, then experience can rewire it. Slowly. Imperfectly. But really.

Second, eating in a state of calm matters more than what you eat. If you're eating while stressed, your body diverts blood flow away from digestion and toward your muscles for fight or flight. That means your gut can't do its job properly. Even if you're eating the healthiest food on the planet, if you're eating it while anxious, you're not digesting it well. Take three slow breaths before your first bite. Put your fork down between bites. Let your body know it's safe to digest.

Third, movement that isn't exercise. I know, I hate that word too. But gentle, rhythmic movement like walking, shaking, or even just rocking can help your gut release the tension it's holding. Peter Levine calls this "pendulation," moving between activation and settling. Your gut needs to learn that it can tighten and then release, over and over again, without getting stuck.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Levine's work on how animals naturally release trauma from their bodies can give you a whole new perspective on why your gut feels the way it does.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). It goes deep into how the body, including the gut, holds the story of our trauma and how we can gently unwind it.

The uncomfortable truth about gut healing and trauma

I'm going to be direct with you. There are a lot of people selling gut healing as a cure for anxiety. They'll tell you to cut out gluten, take thirty supplements, do a parasite cleanse, and you'll be anxiety-free. I've tried versions of that. And it helped a little. But it didn't touch the core of my trauma.

Because here's the thing: your gut is not the enemy. It's not broken. It's doing exactly what it learned to do to survive. It's keeping you on alert because somewhere deep down, your nervous system still believes that if it relaxes, something terrible will happen. That's not a bacterial imbalance. That's a trauma response. And it deserves compassion, not another protocol.

Gabor Mate, the physician who writes about trauma and addiction, says that the question isn't "what's wrong with you?" but "what happened to you?" Your gut symptoms are not a pathology. They are a story. A story of a body that learned to brace for impact. And that story can change, but it changes slowly, with patience, with gentleness, and with the willingness to feel what you've been avoiding.

Your anxiety is not just in your head. It's in your gut, in your cells, in the way your body learned to hold itself. And healing that connection means all of it, not just the parts that are easy to talk about.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your stomach tighten? Did you feel a wave of something? That's your gut-brain axis talking. It's not broken. It's just trying to protect you. And maybe, just maybe, you can start to let it know that you're safe now.

Sit with that.
